coredata.dc:title: Heat transfer characteristics of ceramic foam/molten salt composite phase change material (CPCM) for medium-temperature thermal energy storage
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuai
Yao, Yuanpeng
Jin, Yingai
Shang, Zhen
Yan, Yuying
coredata.dc:description: Molten salts have been widely used as energy storage materials in medium- and high-temperature thermal energy storage. However, pure salt commonly suffers from low thermal conductivity and many conventional methods of heat transfer enhancement do not apply due to the serious corrosion and the extremely high temperature. In the current study, the open-cell SiC ceramic foam was integrated with solar salt (60wt% NaNO3 + 40wt% KNO3) to enhance the heat transfer of salt and avoid severe corrosion issues. A visualised experiment was for the first time conducted to investigate the melting phase change heat transfer in the ceramic foam/molten salt composite phase change material (CPCM). A representative elementary volume (REV)-scale simulation was simultaneously performed and the computational results were compared with experimental data. It is found that the conduction-friendly ceramic skeleton remarkably enhances the heat transfer in molten salt, especially in the region far away from the heat source. The spatial temperature difference across the composite is decreased in both horizontal and vertical directions and the local superheating is mitigated. The enhancement of heat conduction is greater than the suppression of natural convection; as a result, the melting rate of the CPCM is increased by 41.3%. This study provides crucial benchmark data of phase change heat transfer for medium-temperature thermal energy storage and paves the way for system design and optimization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change heat transfer
Molten salt
Ceramic
Medium temperature
Visualization
Thermal energy storage

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFA0702300), H2020-MSCA-RISE-778104–ThermaSMART, Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2019B10042 ), doctoral degree scholarship of China Scholarship Council (CSC). Y. Yao would also like to acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China through grant no. 52006133 , and the International Postdoctoral Exchange Fellowship Program by the Office of China Postdoctoral Council (No. PC2020051). We are grateful for access to the University of Nottingham's Augusta HPC service.

The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFA0702300), H2020-MSCA-RISE-778104–ThermaSMART, Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2019B10042), doctoral degree scholarship of China Scholarship Council (CSC). Y. Yao would also like to acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China through grant no. 52006133, and the International Postdoctoral Exchange Fellowship Program by the Office of China Postdoctoral Council (No. PC2020051). We are grateful for access to the University of Nottingham's Augusta HPC service.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation process of porous SiC ceramic skeleton.
(a) Schematic of the experimental setup; (b) thermocouples arrangement.
Computational domain for the numerical simulation.
(a) Grid independence test; (b) time independence test.
Experimental and simulated solid-liquid phase interface of (a) ceramic foam/solar salt CPCM and (b) pure solar salt.
Solid-liquid phase interface at the plane of x = 10 mm and flow streamlines of (a) ceramic/solar salt CPCM and (b) pure solar salt at 3600 s.

Temperature fields of the front surface of the cavity containing (a) ceramic/molten salt CPCM and (b) pure molten salt. The top figures are the experimental results; the bottom figures are the simulated results.
Variation of the measured temperature at different points. “pure” and “composite” refer to the pure PCM and the composite PCM respectively.
Temperature difference between points P1 and P2/P3. “pure” and “composite” refer to the pure PCM and the composite PCM respectively.
Simulated temperature fields of (a) PCM and (b) ceramic foam in CPCM (the plane of y = 25 mm) and (c) the temperature difference field at 5400 s.
Solid-liquid phase interface of (a) ceramic/solar salt CPCM and (b) pure solar salt at 3000 s without/with considering natural convection.
Variation of the melting fraction with time. “composite” and “pure” refer to composite PCM and pure PCM respectively.

Contribution of natural convection to the melting of ceramic foam/molten salt CPCM. The insets are solid/liquid phase fields and the corresponding temperature fields at t = 1200s, 2700s, 4200 s, 5600 s and 6300 s.
Stored latent heat and sensible heat energy of pure molten salt and ceramic foam/molten salt composite phase change material.
Total stored energy, melting time and thermal energy storage rate of pore molten salt and molten salt/ceramic foam composite phase change material.

row.entry: Pore density of the skeleton, PPI

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermo-physical properties of solar salt, ceramic and quartz.

head.ce:title: Heat transfer characteristics of ceramic foam/molten salt composite phase change material (CPCM) for medium-temperature thermal energy storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molten salts have been widely used as energy storage materials in medium- and high-temperature thermal energy storage. However, pure salt commonly suffers from low thermal conductivity and many conventional methods of heat transfer enhancement do not apply due to the serious corrosion and the extremely high temperature. In the current study, the open-cell SiC ceramic foam was integrated with solar salt (60wt% NaNO3 + 40wt% KNO3) to enhance the heat transfer of salt and avoid severe corrosion issues. A visualised experiment was for the first time conducted to investigate the melting phase change heat transfer in the ceramic foam/molten salt composite phase change material (CPCM). A representative elementary volume (REV)-scale simulation was simultaneously performed and the computational results were compared with experimental data. It is found that the conduction-friendly ceramic skeleton remarkably enhances the heat transfer in molten salt, especially in the region far away from the heat source. The spatial temperature difference across the composite is decreased in both horizontal and vertical directions and the local superheating is mitigated. The enhancement of heat conduction is greater than the suppression of natural convection; as a result, the melting rate of the CPCM is increased by 41.3%. This study provides crucial benchmark data of phase change heat transfer for medium-temperature thermal energy storage and paves the way for system design and optimization.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Velocity in x, y, z direction
Additional term in the momentum equation
The lower limit of melting point
Specific heat capacity of porous skeleton
Wall thickness of the quartz cavity
Parameters used in calculating viscous resistance coefficient

ce:section.ce:para: Using renewable energy is one of the most effective methods to cope with the worldwide energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–3]. Some energy resources, such as solar energy, have the drawbacks of intermittence and instability, which hampers their efficiency seriously [4]. To address this problem, latent heat thermal energy storage (LHTES) was proposed [5–7]. This technology stores thermal energy in phase change materials (PCMs) and releases it when the energy is needed [8,9]. According to the melting point of PCMs, thermal energy storage is classified as low-temperature (melting point < 100 °C), medium-temperature (melting point 100–300 °C) and high-temperature (melting point > 300 °C) ones [10]. Molten salts have been widely used as medium- and high-temperature energy storage materials due to their appropriate melting point, large energy storage density, low cost, excellent thermal stability, etc. [11–14]. In integrated organic Rankine cycle (ORC) systems where solar heat is converted into power, heat transfer fluid (HTF) absorbs heat in the solar collector and flows to the molten salt tank where heat is stored. The storage tank supplies stable heat to the ORC sub-system, maintaining ORC operating under design conditions and overcoming the instability of solar radiation. Moreover, the molten salt storage tank supplies heat for the evening power generation to meet the evening peak electricity demand, which overcomes the intermittency of solar radiation [15].

However, almost all pure PCMs except metals suffer from low thermal conductivity [13]. For molten salt, the low thermal conductivity means a slow charging/discharging rate, which impairs the efficiency of solar power plants and decreases the output power. Many methods have been proposed to enhance the heat transfer of pure PCMs. For low-temperature PCMs such as paraffin, the metal foam and fins have been extensively studied and proved to be good thermal promoters [4]. But for molten salt, these heat transfer enhancement technologies may not apply because the metal foam and fin can be corroded by molten salt [16], and they can soften in the extremely high-temperature environment. In addition, the cost of metal is high, which also impedes its application in molten salt.

Ceramics have inherently high thermal conductivity and excellent corrosion-resistant performance [17–19]. Some researchers have studied using ceramics to enhance the heat transfer of molten salts. Liu et al. [20] prepared a ceramic-based composite phase change material (CPCM) where mullite-corundum act as the porous skeleton and Na2SO4 as PCM. Jiang et al. [21] designed three Na2SO4 NaCl/ceramic CPCMs. They found that the addition of α-alumina and mullite mitigated the supercooling of the eutectic Na2SO4 NaCl. Furthermore, these composites had good thermal stability under 20 thermal cycles. Wang et al. [22] fabricated a ternary salt/Si3N4 CPCM using the pressure impregnation method. Their results indicated that the thermal conductivity of the composite was more than six times higher than that of pure ternary salt. Xu et al. [23] utilised the diatomite to load NaNO3. However, the composite was found to have a low thermal conductivity of ∼0.5 W/m K. Authors added 10 wt% graphite and the thermal conductivity was improved by six-folds. Jiang et al. [24] developed a modified diatomite-based CPCM. CaCO3 powders were added into diatomite and the mixture was sintered at 1150 °C to obtain the porous skeleton. The prepared CPCM exhibited excellent cycling stability during 500 thermal cycles. It is noted that the above mentioned ceramic-based composites are shape-stabilised PCMs; in other words, the pore size is very small, in the magnitude of micron and nanometre, so the fluid is restricted by capillary force and surface tension and the leakage is prevented. In terms of open-cell ceramic skeleton, Liu et al. [25] used the foaming method to prepare a large-pore ceramic-based CPCM where the maximum pore size was in the magnitude of a millimetre. The open-cell configuration enables natural convection and benefits the heat transfer enhancement. The thermal conductivity of prepared CPCM is reported to be 31.8–52.6 W/m K. Luo et al. [26] fabricated nanoparticle-molten salt-ceramic CPCM with synergetic enhanced specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity and optical absorption. The specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity of CPCM are 4.9% and 159% higher than that of pure salt respectively. However, most of the studies on molten salt/ceramic CPCM paid more attention to material preparation and characterisation, while the phase change heat transfer characteristics, such as solid-liquid phase interface, melting rate and heat conduction/natural convection, are rarely investigated.

Phase change heat transfer is of fundamental importance for determining thermal-fluidic behaviours of PCMs and optimising the performance of thermal energy storage (TES) systems [4]. Some researchers have studied the phase change heat transfer characteristics of porous CPCMs. Li et al. [27] experimentally and numerically studied the melting phase change heat transfer of paraffin saturated in open-cell metal foams. The effect of foam morphology including porosity and pore size on the wall temperature and temperature uniformity was investigated. Zheng et al. [28] studied the effect of heating position on the phase change heat transfer of paraffin/copper foam CPCM. They found that PCM heated at the top melted slowest and the temperature difference was the largest. Yao et al. [29] carried out a pore-scale study on the melting heat transfer of paraffin/copper foam. The solid-liquid interface and temperature field were captured to visualise the detailed melting phenomenon. Yang et al. [30] numerically investigated the melting phase change of open-cell foam embedded paraffin. Their results indicate that natural convection exerts a significant influence on the melting front shape, temperature distribution and melting rate.

Although there are extensive studies on the phase change heat transfer of PCMs, studies on heat transfer characteristics of ceramic foam-enhanced molten salt are rarely reported, especially the visualised ones [4]. In the current study, the open-cell SiC ceramic foam was integrated with solar salt (60 wt% NaNO3 + 40 wt% KNO3) to enhance the heat transfer of salt. A visualised experiment was conducted to investigate the phase change heat transfer characteristics of the ceramic foam/molten salt CPCM, including solid-liquid interface, temperature field and melting rate. The heat transfer experiment of pure solar salt was also conducted to make a comparison. In addition, a representative elementary volume (REV)-scale simulation was performed to further understand the thermal-fluidic transport process. Molten salts are suitable latent heat storage media in solar thermal power generation applications [15,31–33], where the ceramic foam/molten salt CPCM with enhanced heat transfer performance can be a promising candidate. This study aims to provide heat transfer information for medium-temperature thermal energy storage and pave the way for the design and optimization of the latent heat energy storage system.

The porous SiC ceramic foam was prepared using the dipping method. The schematic of the preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 . First, the polyurethane (PU) foam (Yueyang Sponge Co., Ltd) with the pore size of 10 PPI was submerged in NaOH solution for 12 h to activate the surface [34]. Then, it was dipped in the SiC ceramic slurry. The slurry consists of SiC powder (particle size:10–13 μm, Xianfeng Material Co., Ltd), kaolin, alumina, yttrium oxide (Yousuo Chemical Co., Ltd), carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC), sodium polyacrylate, silica sol, polyvinyl alcohol (Yousuo Chemical Co., Ltd), N-octanol (Kermel Chemical Co., Ltd) and water. The mass ratio of SiC, kaolin, alumina and yttrium oxide is 86: 7: 4: 3. The mass fraction of these solid powders is 77.9%. CMC, sodium polyacrylate, N-octanol were used as the tackifier, dispersant and defoamer respectively while silica sol and polyvinyl alcohol were employed to improve the adhesion of the slurry to PU foams. The mass fractions of CMC, sodium polyacrylate, N-octanol, silica sol and polyvinyl alcohol are 17.8%, 0.2%, 0.1%, 3.7% and 0.3% respectively.

The foams which were coated by the ceramic slurry were dried at 80 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, they were transferred into a furnace and sintered at 1400 °C for 2 h. The cooling procedure was: the temperature decreased from 1400 °C to 1000 °C within 60 min, then to 400 °C within 200 min and finally to room temperature naturally. The photograph of the final ceramic skeleton is presented in Fig. 1. The porosity of the ceramic foam is 0.85.

NaNO3 and KNO3 (China National Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd) were used as the raw materials to prepare the solar salt. The ceramic foam was placed in a quartz cavity. The solar salt was heated to melt and then poured into the cavity. After cooling, the ceramic foam/molten salt CPCM was obtained. The dimension of CPCM is 50 × 50 × 50 mm and the impregnation ratio is above 95%.

The schematic of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The setup comprises four parts: the test section, heating system, data acquisition system and visualization module. The whole test section was covered by thermal insulation materials to reduce the heat loss to the surroundings. An aluminium plate was used to heat the test section and controlled by a temperature controller (AI-516P, Yudian Technology Co., Ltd). Three K-type thermocouples (WRNK-191, Jiayi Electric Co., Ltd) with a data collection system (KSA12A0R, Keshun Instrument Co., Ltd) were employed to record the local temperature. An infrared camera (HM-TPH16–6VF/W, Hikvision Technology Co., Ltd) and a digital camera were utilised to capture the temperature field and solid-liquid phase interface.

The PCM was enclosed in the quartz cavity with a wall thickness of 2 mm. A gap of 5 mm was left between the top surface of PCM and the cavity to allow the thermal expansion of PCM. During the experiment, the quartz cavity was heated by the left aluminium heater

Three small holes were drilled in the centre of the PCM. The depth is 25 mm and the distance from the left wall of the cavity is 5 mm (point P1), 25 mm (point P2) and 45 mm (point P3) respectively. Thermocouples were inserted into the holes to measure the local temperature at points P1, P2 and P3. The thermocouples were calibrated against a standard thermometer. The distribution of thermocouples is presented in Fig. 2(b).

An infrared camera was used to capture the temperature field of the front surface of the quartz cavity. The spectral range of the infrared camera is 8–14 μm while the field of view is 25 × 19° The optical resolution is 1600 × 1200 pixels and the noise equivalent temperature difference is 0.05 K. The infrared camera was calibrated using a pre-calibrated thermocouple. During the calibration process, the temperature ranged from 25 °C to 300 °C, which covered the temperature range in the actual experiment. By comparing the infrared-based temperature and the thermocouple-based measurement [35], the surface emissivity of the quartz cavity was determined to be 0.956.

During the experiment, the aluminium heater was controlled at a constant temperature of 300 °C. The accuracy of the temperature controller of the aluminium electrical heater is ±0.25%. The digital camera was used to record the solid-liquid phase interface. The front insulation material was removed every 10 min to obtain the phase image of the melting process. The melting experiment of pure solar salt was also conducted to make a comparison. The uncertainties of all parameters are summarised in Table 1 .

A REV-scale simulation was performed to obtain some detailed heat transfer information and assist in understanding the melting phase change heat transfer [4]. The REV-scale simulation treats the porous CPCM as a uniformly mixed medium and does not require an accurate description of the porous skeleton. In other words, it ignores the complex geometric information of the medium. This method uses some statistical parameters, such as porosity, permeability and effective thermal conductivity, to characterize the porous structure. The disadvantage is that it cannot reveal the flow and heat transfer characteristics in pores. But due to the simple input and low computational cost, it is a powerful tool for simulating large-scale systems [36]. The modelling process will be introduced in the following subsections.

The two-temperature model considers the thermal non-equilibrium between PCM and the porous skeleton [4]. It was used in the current simulation to model the phase change heat transfer and flow process in ceramic foam/solar salt CPCM. Before establishing the model, the following assumptions were made: (1) the porous skeleton and PCM are homogenous and isotropic; (2) the flow of liquid PCM is laminar and incompressible; (3) the liquid PCM is subjected to the Boussinesq approximation and (4) the volume difference of PCM before/after melting is neglected.
The governing equations in three-dimensional space are summarised as follows:

Continuity equation [27]:
(1)

∂ t

U →
)
= 0
Since the flow occurs in the liquid region, the continuity equation is only established for the liquid. And the flow is assumed incompressible, Eq. (1) reduces to [4]:
(2)
∇ ·
U →
= 0

To account for the existence of porous skeleton, a series of semi-empirical correlations have been developed to model the flow in porous skeleton [4]. Momentum equations are given by [37]:

where

is the fluid density;

is the porosity; u, v and w are the velocities in x, y and z directions, respectively (assuming that the gravity is along z direction); the second term on the right side of momentum equations accounts for the Darcy effect; the third term is the inertial resistance term, K is the permeability which equals the inverse of the viscous resistance coefficient; the fourth term is the flow resistance term (Brinkman term) [9]; the fifth term on the right side of Eq. (5) denotes the natural convection driven by temperature difference; the last term is the superficial velocity source term to discriminate the solid-liquid region [38].

The two-temperature model considers the temperature difference between the PCM and the skeleton [4] and is used in the current simulation. In the two-temperature model, the energy equations for PCM and skeleton are formulated separately. The energy equations are: for PCM [38]:

where

are the effective thermal conductivity of PCM and the skeleton, respectively;

is the volumetric interfacial heat transfer coefficient; c pf and c ps are the specific heat of PCM and the skeleton respectively; L is the latent heat; f l is the liquid fraction.

Table 2 summarised the thermo-physical properties of solar salt, ceramic and quartz. Discovery DSC25 (TA Instruments Co., Ltd) was used to measure the melting point, latent heat and specific heat. The accuracy of the temperature control of the equipment is ± 0.01 °C. The accuracy of the calorimeter (indium, the standard metal) is ± 0.1%. The baseline reproducibility is < 40 μW. Thermal conductivity was measured using Hot Disk 2500S (Hot Disk AB Co., Ltd). The accuracy is ± 3%. Other parameters are adopted from [39].

The effective thermal conductivity is calculated using the extended Lemlich model [6]:
(8)
k se
=
1 −
ε
3
k s
(9)
k fe

2 +
ε
3
k f
(10)
k e
=
k fe
+
k se
where
k s
and
k f
are the thermal conductivity of pure ceramic and PCM, respectively.

Eqs. (8)–(10) were developed based on the direct simulation of thermal conduction over a wide range of thermal conductivity ratios (0.001–1000) between the porous skeleton and filling medium. There are some other models for calculating effective thermal conductivity, such as Calmidi & Mahajan [40], Dai et al. [41] and Yao et al. [42]. Correlations (8)–(10) have been compared with these models and experimental data and show high accuracy [6]. Moreover, the correlations have a simple and elegant form of expression and can be very easily used in modelling. Therefore, they were adopted in the current simulation to predict effective thermal conductivity.

The volumetric interfacial heat transfer coefficient is calculated by [43]:
(11)

Eqs. (11) and (12) were specifically developed to investigate the phase change heat transfer characteristics of PCM in open-cell foam where the natural convection of PCM is weak [43]. These correlations specifically address the low Reynolds number (0–1) flow. They cover both the diffusion-dominated interstitial heat transfer region (Re ≤ 0.1) and convection-dominated interstitial heat transfer region (0.1 < Re ≤ 1).

The viscous resistance coefficient ϑ and inertial resistance coefficient F l are calculated by [9]:

These correlations are suitable for the solid-liquid phase change of PCM in porous foam [9].

The computational domain in the numerical study is shown in Fig. 3 . The dimension of the PCM domain is 50 × 50 × 50 mm and the wall thickness of the quartz cavity is 2 mm. The dimension of the computational domain is consistent with the real experimental configuration. The coordinate system of the PCM domain is (x: 0–50; y: 0–50; z: 0–50, unit: mm) while that of the entire computation domain is (x: −2–52; y: −2–52; z: −2–50, unit: mm). During the simulation, the left side of the quartz cavity is set with a constant temperature of 300 °C while other sides are set as adiabatic.

For the entire computational domain, the initial conditions are:
(18)
u = v = w = 0 , T =
T i
where T i is the initial temperature (27 °C).

where T con is the constant heating temperature (300 °C).

)
· n
| i
=
(
−
k s
∇
T s
)
· n
| i
,
(
−
k qz
∇
T qz
)
· n
| i
=
(
−
k f
∇
T f
)
· n
| i
where i represents the planes of x = 0, x = 50, y = 0, y = 50 and z = 0.

The numerical simulation was performed using ANSYS Fluent 18.0 package. The governing equations were discretized using the finite volume method. The SIMPLE scheme was employed to couple pressure and velocity. The second-order upwind method was implemented to discretise pressure, momentum and energy terms. The under-relaxation factors for pressure, density, body forces, momentum, liquid fraction update and energy were set as 0.3, 1, 1, 0.7, 0.9 and 1 respectively. The convergence criteria for conservation equations of continuity, momentum and energy were 10−4, 10−4 and 10−8 respectively.

Several mesh sets (1959,182 cells, 5175,550 cells and 11,521,241 cells) and time steps (0.2 s, 0.5 s and 1 s) were tested to balance the computational accuracy and time. First, under the fixed time step of 0.5 s, three mesh sets were tested and it is found from Fig. 4 a that the maximum deviation in the liquid fraction is less than 0.03. Subsequently, under the fixed mesh of 5175,550 cells, three time steps were tested and the maximum deviation is less than 0.01 (Fig. 4b). The mesh set of 5175,550 cells and the time step of 0.5 s were chosen as the final settings.

Fig. 5 shows the experimental and simulated solid-liquid phase interface (the plane of y = 25 mm). It is seen that for both CPCM and pure PCM, the solid-liquid phase interface is curved. Combined with the simulated flow fields in Fig. 6 , it can be inferred that PCM near the heating wall is melted first, afterwards, the liquid hot PCM is driven by the buoyancy force and flows upwards along the heating wall; the hot PCM accumulates at the top region and accelerates the melting of this region, while at the bottom region, the melting is slow; as a result, a sloped phase interface is formed. With the time elapsed, more PCM is melted and the effect of natural convection is strengthened, so the solid-liquid interface becomes more curved.

Compared to pure salt, the experimental solid-liquid interface of CPCM is less curved. This should be attributed to the weak natural convection: the addition of ceramic foam suppresses natural convection; and there are some dead-end pores in the ceramic foam, which further restricts natural convection.

By comparing the experimental and numerical results, it is found that the simulated melting is faster than the experimental result. The possible reasons are as follows: (1) due to the surface roughness, the aluminium heater and the quartz cavity cannot be fit completely, which causes the contact thermal resistance and decreases the heating efficiency; while in the simulation, the left side of the quartz cavity is constantly 300 °C; (2) the aluminium silicate fiberboards with the thickness of 5.0 cm were used to wrap the quartz cavity in the experiment, however, there may be still a little heat loss to the environment; (3) during the experiment, the front cover was removed every 10 min to take pictures, which also leads to heat loss; (4) the temperature controller is set as 300 °C, however, it takes some time for the aluminium heater to reach 300 °C, while in the simulation, the left side of the quartz cavity is 300 °C initially. Thus, it is expected that the simulated melting is faster than the experimental measurements. Nevertheless, the shape and the evolution trend of the simulated phase interface is consistent with experimental results. Thus, the numerical results can still be used to analyse the heat transfer characteristics.

Compared to conventional paraffin/metal foam CPCM [44], the solid-liquid phase interface of molten salt/ceramic foam CPCM is much curved. The reason may lie in the low viscosity of molten salt: the viscosity of liquid molten salt is 4.6 mPa·s, while that of paraffin is 32.6 mPa·s [44]. As a result, the natural convection in molten salt/ceramic foam CPCM is stronger and solid-liquid interface is more curved.

The simulated solid-liquid phase interface at the plane of x = 10 mm is presented in Fig. 6. Due to the high thermal diffusivity of the quartz, the melting rate of the edge region is faster than that of the middle region. The velocity magnitude of the CPCM is obviously smaller than that of the pure PCM, where the maximum velocity of the liquid PCM in the former is about 9 × 10−4 m/s while that in the latter can reach up to 2.2 × 10−3 m/s. This is because, in the pure PCM, the liquid PCM flows freely while in the CPCM, the fluid flow is restricted by the inertial and viscous resistance induced by the porous skeleton [45]. The low velocity of the liquid PCM means that the natural convection is weakened. As seen in Fig. 5, the bottom region of the liquid salt in CPCM is wider than that in pure PCM, which confirms natural convection is weaker in CPCM. Nevertheless, the seriously sloped interface of the CPCM suggests that natural convection still plays a non-negligible role in heat transfer. It may be attributed to the open cells and large pores of the ceramic foam. The addition of the ceramic foam obviously accelerates the melting. The complete melting time of CPCM is 7200 s while that of pure PCM is 12600s. The melting rate is calculated by:
(25)
v m
=
V PCM
t m
where V PCM is the volume of molten salt; t m is the complete melting time.

Considering the porosity (0.85) and the impregnation ratio (> 95%), the melting rate of CPCM is 41.3% higher than that of pure PCM. Moreover, it is seen from the simulated phase fields that the mushy region of CPCM is wider than that of pure PCM, indicating that the spatially synchronous phase change is boosted. The mushy region is the region where the temperature decreases from the liquidus temperature (246.0 °C) to the solidus temperature (222.9 °C). The thermal resistance of pure PCM is high, so the temperature quickly decreases from the liquidus temperature to the solidus temperature, leading to a narrow mushy region. In contrast, the effective thermal conductivity of CPCM is higher and the thermal resistance is lower, so the high temperature can extend to the further area, resulting in a wider mushy region.

It should be pointed out that the experimental solid-liquid phase interface in Fig. 5(a) is observed through the front window. The difference between the front and internal solid-liquid interface is inevitable due to dead-end pores. In the current study, the internal solid-liquid interface is assumed to be similar to the front one as the ceramic foam is overall uniform. And when the front salt was completely melted and the thermal insulation was removed, we observed through the right and back windows that the salt was also completely melted.

The transient temperature distributions on the front surface of the cavity with CPCM or pure PCM is presented in Fig. 7 . It is seen that both the experimental and simulated temperature fields are consistent with the solid-liquid phase interface. And the reasons for the deviation between the experimental and numerical results have been analysed in the Section 4.1. It is found from Fig. 7 that the thermal stratification of pure PCM is very obvious; moreover, the high-temperature region is concentrated in the upper left corner, resulting in the local superheating. In contrast, the temperature distribution of the CPCM is more uniform; the spatial temperature difference is decreased in both horizontal and vertical directions. The thermal conductivity of pure solar salt (solid) is 0.59 W/m K; the effective thermal conductivity of CPCM is 1.60 W/m K. The addition of ceramic foam effectively enhances the transfer heat of salt, so the temperature of salt, especially the part far away from the heating wall increases faster. Moreover, since the thermal energy of the heat source can be efficiently transferred to the internal salt, the local superheating is mitigated.

The temperature of points P1, P2 and P3 measured by thermocouples is plotted in Fig. 8 . It is seen that the further away from the heating wall, the slower the temperature rises. This is understandable because of the further the distance, the larger the thermal resistance. After adding the ceramic foam, the temperature of all the points rises faster. It is noted that the improvement in temperature is more obvious for points P2 and P3. It may be attributed to the fact that P2 and P3 are more distant from the heating wall, so the enhancement effect of the ceramic skeleton can be exhibited more fully.

The temperature difference between P1 and P2/P3 is plotted in Fig. 9 . The temperature difference curves have the characteristics of “rising first, then falling and finally stabilizing”. This is because the temperature of P1 increases faster than the other two points, so the temperature difference becomes larger at the initial stage; when the temperature of P1 gradually approaches the maximum value, that of P2 and P3 still increases rapidly, thus the temperature difference gets smaller; when the PCM which covers P1, P2 and P3 is completely melted, heat can be transferred effectively from P1 to P2/P3 under the effect of heat conduction plus natural convection, so the temperature difference becomes stable.

It is noted that the addition of the ceramic foam decreases the temperature difference significantly. Taking the temperature difference between P1 and P2 as an example, the maximum value in the pure PCM is 125.7 °C while that in the composite PCM is 98.8 °C, the decrease is about 27 °C. The temperature uniformity is very important because the uniform temperature distribution can mitigate the thermal stress on the equipment. The addition of the ceramic foam not only improves the energy storage efficiency but also benefits extending the lifespan of equipment.

Fig. 10 shows the temperature fields of PCM and ceramic foam in CPCM (T ceramic - T PCM). It is seen that an obviously high temperature-difference region forms in the bottom left corner. This is due to the fact that the hot liquid PCM in the bottom left corner has been pushed to the top region and the cold PCM flows to this region; the temperature of PCM is low, so the temperature difference is large. The maximum temperature difference is about 0.52 °C. In the experiment, thermocouples measured the temperature without distinguishing PCM and the skeleton; since the temperature difference is much smaller than the local temperature, this measurement method is acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contributions of natural convection and heat conduction
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the contributions of heat conduction and natural convection to the phase change heat transfer, the simulation without considering the natural convection was performed. Fig. 11 shows the solid-liquid phase interface without/with considering the natural convection. For the case without considering natural convection, the heat transfer mechanism is only the heat conduction, so the phase interface is always parallel to the heating wall.

The contributions of heat conduction and natural convection to melting are quantitatively compared in Fig. 12 . It is seen that before 2000s, heat conduction dominates the heat transfer in both pure PCM and CPCM; with the elapse of time, natural convection becomes more significant. However, the role of natural convection is different for pure PCM and CPCM. In the case of pure PCM, the natural convection gradually becomes the dominated heat transfer mechanism while for CPCM, heat conduction is always the dominated heat transfer mechanism.

The contribution of natural convection to the melting of CPCM is plotted in Fig. 13 . The data is obtained by subtracting the melting fraction due to heat conduction from the total melting fraction at each time step. It is seen that the melting induced by natural convection increases with time, which may be attributed to the fact that the solid-liquid interface area increases as more PCM is melted. When the solid-liquid interface crosses the right wall, the interface area begins to decrease, however, the melting fraction continues to increase. It may be because the temperature of solid PCM increases due to heat conduction, which causes solid PCM melted more easily. The melting induced by natural convection does not always increase. At the final stage, although the temperature of solid PCM is high, the solid-liquid interface area becomes very small; natural convection is weak and the corresponding melting fraction decreases.

Fig. 14 shows the simulated stored energy of pure molten salt and CPCM. The stored energy consists of two parts: sensible heat energy and latent heat energy. The former is induced by the temperature increase of material while the latter is induced by the phase change of molten salt. It is seen from Fig. 14 that the sensible heat energy is much larger than the latent heat energy. This is due to the large temperature increment: the initial temperature is 27 °C; the final volume-averaged temperature, namely the temperature when the salt is completely melted, of pure PCM and CPCM is 289 °C and 282 °C respectively. The temperature increment is 262 °C and 255 °C respectively, leading to the large sensible heat energy.

It is noted that both the sensible heat energy and the latent heat energy of CPCM rise faster than that of pure salt. This should be attributed to the faster temperature increase, as indicated in Fig. 8. As the sensible heat storage results from the temperature increase of material, the faster the temperature rises, the faster the sensible heat energy is stored; moreover, the faster temperature increase leads to a higher melting rate and thus the faster latent heat storage.

The energy storage rate is calculated by [46]:
(26)
w =
E
t m
where E is the total stored energy; t m is the complete melting time.

It is seen from Fig. 15 that the total stored energy of CPCM is lower than that of pure salt. CPCM contains only 85% molten salt and its final temperature is lower due to the shorter heating time. But its energy storage rate is improved by 40.3% than pure molten salt. Ceramic foam shortens the melting time and enhances the comprehensive energy storage performance.

In the current study, the porous SiC ceramic foam was integrated with solar salt and the melting phase change heat transfer in the composite phase change material (CPCM) was studied. A visualised experiment was conducted to capture the solid-liquid phase interface, temperature field, etc. In addition, a representative elementary volume (REV)-scale simulation was performed to provide detailed heat transfer and flow information. The following conclusions are drawn:
(1)
Using ceramic foam is an effective method to enhance the heat transfer of molten salt. In the current experiment, the melting rate of molten salt/ceramic foam CPCM is increased by 41.3% compared to pure molten salt.
(2)
The temperature field of CPCM is more uniform. The maximum temperature difference is decreased from 125.7 °C to 98.8 °C. The enhancement effect of ceramic foam is more significant in the region far away from the heat source.
(3)
Natural convection gradually dominates the heat transfer in pure salt while heat conduction is always the dominated heat transfer mechanism in CPCM. The enhancement of heat conduction is greater than the suppression of natural convection in CPCM, which leads to the overall enhancement of heat transfer.
(4)
Ceramic foam accelerates both sensible heat storage and latent heat storage. The energy storage rate of CPCM is increased by 40.3% compared to pure molten salt.

Shuai Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yuanpeng Yao: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yingai Jin: Resources, Data curation. Zhen Shang: Resources, Data curation. Yuying Yan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFA0702300), H2020-MSCA-RISE-778104–ThermaSMART, Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (No. 2019B10042), doctoral degree scholarship of China Scholarship Council (CSC). Y. Yao would also like to acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China through grant no. 52006133, and the International Postdoctoral Exchange Fellowship Program by the Office of China Postdoctoral Council (No. PC2020051). We are grateful for access to the University of Nottingham's Augusta HPC service.

Solar Energy Mater. Solar Cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effective thermal conductivity of high porosity fibrous metal foams
J. Heat Transfer

Int. Commun. Heat Mass Transfer

Int. J. Thermal Sci.

J. Heat Transfer

Appl. Energy
